title: Vulcan's Mind — public project note
authors: Volodymyr Motornyi
qualityNotes: Author-owned factual project note derived from public docs.

title: Lusterko — public project note
authors: Volodymyr Motornyi
qualityNotes: Author-owned factual project note. Sanitized — no sensitive military details, private health data, or unverified maturity claims.

title: Іменуй мене — public project note
authors: Volodymyr Motornyi
qualityNotes: Author-owned factual project note. Public-facing only; no claims beyond what is publicly described.

title: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models
authors: Bommasani R., Hudson D. A., Adeli E., et al.
summary: Stanford CRFM report mapping capabilities, applications, and risks of large pretrained models that are adapted to many downstream tasks. Useful framing for AI automation reliability and harms.
publisher: Stanford Center for Research on Foundation Models
qualityNotes: Foundational position paper from a major academic group; widely cited.

title: Augmented Language Models: A Survey
authors: Mialon G., Dessì R., Lomeli M., et al.
summary: Survey of how language models are augmented with reasoning, retrieval, and tools — directly relevant to LLM-driven automation patterns.
qualityNotes: Authoritative survey from Meta AI authors covering tool use and retrieval augmentation.

title: AI Agents That Matter
authors: Kapoor S., Stroebl B., Siegel Z., et al.
summary: Critical analysis of AI agent benchmarks and reproducibility. Argues for cost-aware, replicable evaluation rather than leaderboard-driven hype — useful caution for automation reliability claims.
qualityNotes: Provides explicit reproducibility/cost caveats absent in many agent papers.

title: OpenAI Platform — Function Calling guide
authors: OpenAI
summary: Vendor reference for structured tool use with OpenAI models: declaring tool schemas, JSON output, parallel calls, and integration patterns for AI automation.
qualityNotes: Primary vendor docs for the API used by this project.

title: Anthropic — Build with Claude: Tool use
authors: Anthropic
summary: Anthropic's reference for tool use with Claude: defining tools, invoking them, parallel tool calls, and reliability tips. Comparison reference for vendor-neutral automation design.
qualityNotes: Provides cross-vendor perspective alongside the OpenAI guide.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
authors: Lewis P., Perez E., Piktus A., et al.
summary: Original RAG paper combining a parametric seq2seq model with a non-parametric retriever over a Wikipedia dump, improving open-domain QA and reducing hallucination. Foundational reference.
qualityNotes: Founding work that named the RAG paradigm used by this project.

title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
authors: Karpukhin V., Oğuz B., Min S., et al.
summary: Demonstrates that learned dense passage embeddings outperform sparse BM25 for open-domain QA — a key argument for vector-based retrieval over keyword search.
qualityNotes: Key reference for dense embedding retrieval.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey
authors: Gao Y., Xiong Y., Gao X., et al.
summary: Comprehensive survey distinguishing naive, advanced, and modular RAG; covers chunking, retrieval, post-retrieval re-ranking, and evaluation.
qualityNotes: Up-to-date survey useful as a single reference for current RAG practice.

title: Survey of Hallucination in Natural Language Generation
authors: Ji Z., Lee N., Frieske R., et al.
summary: Taxonomy of hallucination types across NLG tasks and overview of mitigation, including grounding and retrieval. Frames the safety motivation for source-aware answers.
qualityNotes: Peer-reviewed ACM survey; widely cited for hallucination taxonomy.

title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
authors: Liu N. F., Lin K., Hewitt J., et al.
summary: Empirical study showing LLM accuracy drops when relevant context is in the middle of a long input — informs how RAG systems should rank and order retrieved chunks.
qualityNotes: Directly motivates per-source diversity and context ordering in retrieval.

title: Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks
authors: Reimers N., Gurevych I.
summary: Introduces Siamese BERT for producing semantically meaningful sentence embeddings suitable for cosine similarity — basis of modern sentence-level retrieval.
qualityNotes: Standard reference for sentence embedding training.

title: An Ultra-Brief Screening Scale for Anxiety and Depression: The PHQ-4
authors: Kroenke K., Spitzer R. L., Williams J. B. W., Löwe B.
summary: Introduces the four-item PHQ-4 combining PHQ-2 and GAD-2 for ultra-brief screening of depression and anxiety in primary care; psychometrics in a US general population sample.
qualityNotes: Primary methodological reference for PHQ-4.

title: The PHQ-9: Validity of a Brief Depression Severity Measure
authors: Kroenke K., Spitzer R. L., Williams J. B. W.
summary: Validation study of the nine-item PHQ-9 depression severity measure in primary care, with cut-points for screening severity. Foundational for digital depression screening.
qualityNotes: Most cited PHQ-9 validation paper.

title: A Brief Measure for Assessing Generalized Anxiety Disorder: The GAD-7
authors: Spitzer R. L., Kroenke K., Williams J. B. W., Löwe B.
summary: Development and validation of the GAD-7 screener for generalized anxiety disorder in primary care; reports cut-point performance and reliability.
qualityNotes: Standard reference for GAD-7.

title: A Global Measure of Perceived Stress
authors: Cohen S., Kamarck T., Mermelstein R.
summary: Original Perceived Stress Scale (PSS) paper — short instrument capturing the degree to which life situations are appraised as stressful, with psychometric properties.
publisher: Journal of Health and Social Behavior
qualityNotes: Original validated PSS reference.

title: Ecological Momentary Assessment
authors: Shiffman S., Stone A. A., Hufford M. R.
summary: Authoritative review of EMA — repeated, in-the-moment self-report — including design choices, biases, compliance, and evidence base for clinical and behavioural research.
qualityNotes: Standard methodological reference for EMA.

title: The Posttraumatic Stress Disorder Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5): Development and Initial Psychometric Evaluation
authors: Blevins C. A., Weathers F. W., Davis M. T., Witte T. K., Domino J. L.
summary: Initial psychometric evaluation of the PCL-5 — the DSM-5-aligned PTSD self-report checklist used widely for screening and symptom severity tracking.
qualityNotes: Primary PCL-5 reference.

title: Combat Duty in Iraq and Afghanistan, Mental Health Problems, and Barriers to Care
authors: Hoge C. W., Castro C. A., Messer S. C., et al.
summary: NEJM landmark study of US service members showing elevated PTSD, depression, and anxiety after combat deployment, plus low rates of help-seeking due to stigma and access barriers.
qualityNotes: Cornerstone study on combat-related mental health and care barriers.

title: Trauma and PTSD in the WHO World Mental Health Surveys
authors: Kessler R. C., Aguilar-Gaxiola S., Alonso J., et al.
summary: Cross-national WHO survey reporting prevalence of trauma exposure and PTSD risk by trauma type across 24 countries — gives the global epidemiological frame for war-related trauma.
qualityNotes: WHO World Mental Health Surveys consortium publication.

title: Moral Injury and Moral Repair in War Veterans: A Preliminary Model and Intervention Strategy
authors: Litz B. T., Stein N., Delaney E., et al.
summary: Conceptual paper distinguishing moral injury from PTSD, with a working clinical model and intervention sketch — defining reference for moral injury in veterans.
qualityNotes: Defining paper for the moral-injury construct.

title: Multiple Session Early Psychological Interventions for the Prevention of Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder
authors: Roberts N. P., Kitchiner N. J., Kenardy J., Robertson L., Lewis C., Bisson J. I.
summary: Cochrane systematic review of multiple-session early psychological interventions for PTSD prevention. Useful as evidence-base reference for what generally does and does not work.
qualityNotes: Cochrane review — top of evidence hierarchy for the question.

title: IASC Guidelines on Mental Health and Psychosocial Support in Emergency Settings
authors: Inter-Agency Standing Committee
summary: Inter-agency consensus guidelines for mental-health and psychosocial support in humanitarian emergencies, including armed conflict — multi-layered intervention pyramid widely used in field response.
qualityNotes: Authoritative inter-agency guideline document.

title: mhGAP Intervention Guide for Mental, Neurological and Substance Use Disorders in Non-Specialized Health Settings, version 2.0
authors: World Health Organization
summary: WHO clinical guidance designed for non-specialist providers in low-resource settings, including disorders relevant in conflict-affected populations such as depression, PTSD, and acute stress.
qualityNotes: Official WHO guideline; highly relevant for austere/post-conflict settings.

title: Tactical Combat Casualty Care (TCCC) Clinical Practice Guidelines
authors: Joint Trauma System / Committee on TCCC
summary: Living set of US DoD Joint Trauma System clinical practice guidelines for prehospital care under combat conditions; the de-facto standard reference for combat casualty care doctrine at a high level.
qualityNotes: Official, public-facing CPG hub. Cite at high level only — no operational/tactical specifics.

title: Death on the Battlefield (2001–2011): Implications for the Future of Combat Casualty Care
authors: Eastridge B. J., Mabry R. L., Seguin P., et al.
summary: Retrospective analysis of US combat fatalities and potentially survivable injuries — drove much of the post-2010 evolution of TCCC priorities (massive haemorrhage, airway, respiration).
publisher: Journal of Trauma and Acute Care Surgery
qualityNotes: High-impact epidemiological study on combat casualty mortality.

title: Eliminating Preventable Death on the Battlefield
authors: Kotwal R. S., Montgomery H. R., Kotwal B. M., et al.
summary: Documents how a Ranger casualty response system using TCCC dramatically reduced preventable battlefield deaths — frequently cited as evidence for systematic, training-driven trauma care.
qualityNotes: Often cited alongside Eastridge 2012; complementary perspective.

title: Causes of Death in U.S. Special Operations Forces in the Global War on Terrorism: 2001–2004
authors: Holcomb J. B., McMullin N. R., Pearse L., et al.
summary: Earlier autopsy-based analysis of causes of death among US special operations forces, used to argue for protocolized prehospital haemorrhage control.
qualityNotes: Historical baseline study cited in TCCC literature.

title: Toward a Theory of Situation Awareness in Dynamic Systems
authors: Endsley M. R.
summary: Classic three-level model of situation awareness (perception, comprehension, projection) — foundational for human-factors design of decision-support and field tools under stress.
qualityNotes: Canonical human-factors paper relevant to austere environments and cognitive load.

title: Evidence-Based Guidelines on the Therapeutic Use of Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (rTMS): An Update (2014–2018)
authors: Lefaucheur J.-P., Aleman A., Baeken C., et al.
summary: International expert guideline summarising evidence-graded rTMS recommendations across psychiatric and neurological indications; key reference for evidence boundaries.
qualityNotes: Latest peer-reviewed European expert consensus guideline for rTMS.

title: Evidence-Based Guidelines on the Therapeutic Use of Transcranial Direct Current Stimulation (tDCS)
authors: Lefaucheur J.-P., Antal A., Ayache S. S., et al.
summary: Companion expert guideline for tDCS — graded evidence per indication. Useful for honestly conveying what tDCS does and does not have evidence to support.
qualityNotes: Pair with rTMS guideline for the canonical evidence picture.

title: Safety of Transcranial Direct Current Stimulation: Evidence Based Update 2016
authors: Bikson M., Grossman P., Thomas C., et al.
summary: Evidence-based safety review of conventional tDCS protocols summarising known adverse events and dose limits — essential reference whenever discussing safety boundaries.
qualityNotes: Anchor reference for tDCS safety claims.

title: Daily Left Prefrontal Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation Therapy for Major Depressive Disorder
authors: George M. S., Lisanby S. H., Avery D., et al.
summary: Sham-controlled multisite trial of daily left dorsolateral prefrontal rTMS for treatment-resistant depression; one of the trials that anchored regulatory acceptance.
qualityNotes: Pivotal trial cited in many subsequent reviews.

title: Efficacy and Safety of Deep Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation for Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
authors: Carmi L., Tendler A., Bystritsky A., et al.
summary: Multicenter sham-controlled trial of deep TMS for OCD targeting medial prefrontal/anterior cingulate, supporting regulatory clearance for that indication.
qualityNotes: Cited indication-specific trial illustrating evidence for non-depression uses.

title: Efficacy of Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation Targets for Depression Is Related to Intrinsic Functional Connectivity with the Subgenual Cingulate
authors: Fox M. D., Buckner R. L., White M. P., Greicius M. D., Pascual-Leone A.
summary: Connectivity-based explanation for why some prefrontal TMS targets work better than others for depression — influential for personalised, network-guided targeting research.
qualityNotes: Important mechanistic/targeting paper.

title: Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation and Cognitive Neuroscience
authors: Walsh V., Cowey A.
summary: Foundational Nature Reviews Neuroscience overview of TMS as a tool for causal cognitive neuroscience — useful conceptual primer outside therapeutic claims.
qualityNotes: Conceptual primer; balances therapy-focused references.

title: Cognitive Load During Problem Solving: Effects on Learning
authors: Sweller J.
summary: Original cognitive load theory paper — distinguishes intrinsic, extraneous, and germane load and their effects on learning. Foundational for instructional and interface design.
qualityNotes: Founding paper for cognitive load theory.

title: A Meta-Analysis of the Impact of Short-Term Sleep Deprivation on Cognitive Variables
authors: Lim J., Dinges D. F.
summary: Meta-analysis quantifying how short-term sleep deprivation degrades attention, executive function, and memory — directly relevant to performance under operational stress.
qualityNotes: Standard meta-analytic reference for sleep deprivation effects.

title: Executive Functions
authors: Diamond A.
summary: Annual Review article distinguishing core executive functions — inhibition, working memory, cognitive flexibility — and their malleability via training and lifestyle.
qualityNotes: Widely cited authoritative review.

title: Test-Enhanced Learning: Taking Memory Tests Improves Long-Term Retention
authors: Roediger H. L., Karpicke J. D.
summary: Demonstrates the testing effect — retrieval practice yields better long-term retention than additional study — a backbone of evidence-based study and training design.
qualityNotes: Standard retrieval-practice reference.

title: Stress and Cognitive Function
authors: McEwen B. S., Sapolsky R. M.
summary: Classic review on glucocorticoids, stress, and cognition — useful background for discussing how chronic stress shapes cognitive performance and learning.
qualityNotes: Older but widely cited mechanistic review.

title: Guidelines for Human-AI Interaction
authors: Amershi S., Weld D., Vorvoreanu M., et al.
summary: 18 generally applicable design guidelines for human-AI interaction — initial, during, when wrong, and over time — derived and validated by Microsoft Research.
qualityNotes: Influential, directly applicable to chatbot UX.

title: Trust in Automation: Designing for Appropriate Reliance
authors: Lee J. D., See K. A.
summary: Foundational human-factors review on trust and reliance in automated systems — distinguishes appropriate reliance, miscalibrated trust, and disuse/misuse patterns.
qualityNotes: Cornerstone reference for trust calibration.

title: Does the Whole Exceed Its Parts? The Effect of AI Explanations on Complementary Team Performance
authors: Bansal G., Wu T., Zhou J., Fok R., Nushi B., Kamar E., Ribeiro M. T., Weld D. S.
summary: Empirical study showing that adding AI explanations does not always improve human–AI team performance — explanations can even induce overreliance. Cautionary reference for explainable AI design.
qualityNotes: Counterweight to naive 'more explanation = more trust' assumptions.

title: To Trust or to Think: Cognitive Forcing Functions Can Reduce Overreliance on AI in AI-Assisted Decision-Making
authors: Buçinca Z., Malaya M. B., Gajos K. Z.
summary: Shows that simple interventions slowing automatic acceptance of AI suggestions reduce overreliance, especially among more confident users — practical lever for chatbot UX.
qualityNotes: Concrete UX patterns for managing overreliance.

title: Formalizing Trust in Artificial Intelligence: Prerequisites, Causes and Goals of Human Trust in AI
authors: Jacovi A., Marasović A., Miller T., Goldberg Y.
summary: Conceptual paper formalising what trust in AI means, what makes it warranted, and how design choices should distinguish trust from mere reliance. Useful theoretical anchor.
qualityNotes: Provides shared vocabulary for trust-related claims.
